input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin systems** studied across the documents span **cyber-biophysical systems** (e.g., interconnected cyber-physical and biological components, such as smart manufacturing environments or automotive systems with embedded sensors and actuators) and **domain-specific industrial applications** (e.g., automotive, aerospace, battery systems, and manufacturing). Key characteristics include:
  - **Automotive Systems**: Physical twins in the automotive domain (e.g., vehicles, assembly lines, or powertrain components) integrate **real-time sensor data** (e.g., IoT-enabled ECUs, LiDAR, cameras) and **cyber-physical interactions** (e.g., V2X communication, adaptive cruise control) to enable **physical-digital convergence** ([8], [9]). Components include **mechatronic subsystems** (e.g., electric motors, battery management systems) and **production-line equipment** (e.g., CNC machines, robotic arms).
  - **Manufacturing Systems**: Aligned with **ISO 23247-1**, these systems are **highly dynamic, data-intensive, and interconnected**, featuring **cyber-physical production systems (CPPS)** with **digital threads** linking design, production, and maintenance ([10], [12]). Key components include **smart factories** (e.g., Industry 4.0 environments), **additive manufacturing (AM) machines**, and **logistics networks** with **RFID/QR code tracking**.
  - **Aerospace Systems**: Physical twins in aerospace (e.g., satellites, spacecraft) focus on **on-orbit collision avoidance** and **space debris detection**, leveraging **high-fidelity sensor networks** (e.g., radar, optical sensors) and **real-time trajectory modeling** ([16]).
  - **Battery Systems**: Physical twins for **lithium-ion batteries** or **energy storage systems** model **degradation mechanisms**, **thermal management**, and **state-of-charge (SoC)/state-of-health (SoH) estimation** using **multi-physics simulations** ([17]).
  - **Cyber-Biophysical Systems**: These systems (e.g., smart grids, healthcare monitoring) combine **biological processes** (e.g., patient vitals) with **cyber components** (e.g., IoT devices, AI-driven diagnostics) to enable **closed-loop control** and **predictive maintenance** ([7]).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT) services** provided across the documents are categorized by **functional domains**, **user roles**, and **technical capabilities**, including:
  - **Monitoring Services**:
    - **Real-time data ingestion** from **IoT sensors** (e.g., temperature, vibration, current) and **edge devices** (e.g., Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson) for **condition monitoring** in manufacturing ([10], [14]).
    - **Anomaly detection** via **machine learning (ML) models** (e.g., LSTM, isolation forests) trained on **historical and streaming data** ([7], [9]).
    - **Predictive maintenance** for **rotating machinery** (e.g., bearings, gears) using **physics-informed neural networks (PINNs)** ([15]).
  - **Optimization Services**:
    - **Closed-loop optimization** of **production schedules** (e.g., job shop scheduling) via **reinforcement learning (RL)** or **genetic algorithms** ([10]).
    - **Energy optimization** in **battery systems** (e.g., SoC balancing, charging/discharging cycles) using **model predictive control (MPC)** ([17]).
    - **Trajectory optimization** for **aerospace assets** (e.g., collision avoidance maneuvers) via **optimal control theory** ([16]).
  - **Prediction Services**:
    - **Failure prognosis** for **automotive components** (e.g., brake systems, powertrains) using **survival analysis** or **Bayesian networks** ([8], [9]).
    - **Degradation modeling** for **battery cells** (e.g., capacity fade prediction) via **Gaussian process regression** ([17]).
    - **Cyber-attack prediction** in **cyber-biophysical systems** using **digital twin-based intrusion detection systems (IDS)** ([7]).
  - **Control Services**:
    - **Adaptive control** for **autonomous vehicles** (e.g., platooning, lane-keeping) via **fuzzy logic controllers** or **deep Q-networks (DQN)** ([8]).
    - **Remote operation** of **manufacturing assets** (e.g., robotic arms) using **digital twin-enabled teleoperation** ([12]).
  - **Visualization Services**:
    - **3D/4D immersive dashboards** (e.g., Unity, Unreal Engine) for **real-time asset tracking** and **what-if analysis** ([10], [14]).
    - **Augmented Reality (AR)/Virtual Reality (VR)** for **maintenance training** (e.g., overlaying DT data on physical assets) ([9]).
  - **Target Users**:
    - **Engineers** (e.g., mechanical, electrical) for **design validation** and **troubleshooting**.
    - **Operators** (e.g., plant managers, pilots) for **real-time decision support**.
    - **Maintenance technicians** for **predictive maintenance planning**.
    - **Regulators** (e.g., aviation authorities) for **compliance verification** ([16]).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **tooling and enablers** for Digital Twin implementation across the documents include **standardized frameworks**, **software platforms**, **simulation engines**, and **specialized technologies**, categorized by their roles:
  - **Standardized Frameworks**:
    - **ISO 23247 (Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing)**:
      - **Part 1 (Overview and General Principles)**: Defines **lifecycle phases** (design, operation, retirement), **data exchange protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT), and **interoperability requirements** ([10], [14]).
      - **Adaptation for Aerospace**: Extends ISO 23247 to **on-orbit systems** with **collision avoidance algorithms** and **debris tracking** ([16]).
    - **ISO/IEC 30173 (Digital Twin – Concepts and Terminology)**: Provides **unified terminology** for **digital twin maturity levels** (e.g., Level 0: Static 3D model; Level 4: Fully autonomous) ([11]).
  - **Software Platforms and Development Tools**:
    - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) Tools**:
      - **Eclipse Modeling Framework (EMF)** for **ontology modeling** and **meta-modeling** ([7]).
      - **SysML** for **systems engineering** and **requirements traceability** ([13]).
    - **Simulation Engines**:
      - **COMSOL Multiphysics** for **multi-domain simulations** (e.g., thermal, electromagnetic, fluid dynamics) in **battery systems** ([17]).
      - **ANSYS Fluent**/**Siemens NX** for **CFD and FEA** in **automotive/aerospace** ([8], [16]).
      - **Gazebo/ROS** for **robotics and autonomous systems** ([9]).
    - **Edge Computing Frameworks**:
      - **EdgeX Foundry** for **distributed data processing** in **IoT-enabled DTs** ([14]).
      - **Kubernetes (K8s)** for **containerized DT services** deployment ([12]).
  - **Databases and Data Management**:
    - **Time-Series Databases**:
      - **InfluxDB**/**TimescaleDB** for **high-frequency sensor data** storage ([14]).
    - **Graph Databases**:
      - **Neo4j** for **digital thread modeling** (e.g., linking design, manufacturing, and maintenance data) ([10]).
    - **Knowledge Graphs**:
      - **RDF/OWL ontologies** for **semantic interoperability** between DT components ([7], [13]).
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **AI/ML Libraries**:
      - **TensorFlow/PyTorch** for **deep learning-based predictions** ([15]).
      - **Scikit-learn** for **traditional ML models** (e.g., SVM, Random Forest) ([9]).
    - **Digital Twin-Specific Tools**:
      - **Siemens MindSphere** for **IIoT integration** and **predictive analytics** ([8]).
      - **PTC ThingWorx** for **low-code DT development** ([12]).
      - **NVIDIA Omniverse** for **real-time physics simulations** ([16]).
    - **Communication Protocols**:
      - **OPC UA** for **machine-to-machine (M2M) communication** in **manufacturing DTs** ([10]).
      - **5G/6G** for **ultra-low latency** in **autonomous systems** ([8]).
    - **Cybersecurity**:
      - **Blockchain** for **immutable audit logs** in **cyber-biophysical systems** ([7]).
      - **Zero-Trust Architecture** for **secure DT access** ([14]).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin System (DTS) enforces a **100 ms time synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination, adhering to hardware limitations. This temporal granularity is critical for safety-critical vehicular applications, where stricter synchronization (e.g., <100 ms) is mandated by standards. The system leverages **clock synchronization mechanisms** to ensure alignment across distributed devices, though no explicit mention of synchronization protocols (e.g., NTP, PTP) or frequency-specific latency requirements for individual services is provided. The temporal scale is uniform across the system, with no differentiation between services or entities (e.g., Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User Entity) in terms of granularity or latency constraints beyond the 100 ms baseline.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The DTS adopts a **decentralized, entity-based architecture** with **three distinct hierarchical instances** aligned to ISO 23247 RA: the *Physical Twin Entity*, *Digital Twin Entity*, and *User Entity*. Each entity is deployed on **separate hardware components**, reflecting a **1:1 mapping** between logical entities and physical execution environments. The *Physical Twin Entity* uniquely deviates from ISO 23247 by hosting **general automotive functionality** (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*) alongside resource-specific operations, unlike the standard’s *OME* (Object Management Entity). The *User Entity* is further subdivided into *3D Visualization* and *Control Panel* components under the *User Interface FE*, demonstrating **internal multiplicities** within a single entity. No explicit mention of **multiple Digital Twin instances** (e.g., fleet-wide twins) or dynamic coordination mechanisms between twins is provided. Responsibilities are partitioned by entity: the *Physical Twin Entity* manages **data pre-processing, actuation, and device communication**; the *Digital Twin Entity* handles **data management and control mechanisms**; and the *User Entity* provides **user-facing interfaces**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The DTS orchestrates components using an **ISO 23247 RA-compliant architecture**, structured around **Functional Entities (FEs)** mapped to ROS2 packages for execution. Each FE is implemented as an **independent ROS2 node** within a package, enabling modularity and hardware-agnostic deployment. The system employs a **hybrid centralized-decentralized pattern**: while entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) operate autonomously on distinct hardware, their interactions are governed by **ROS2’s distributed communication framework** (e.g., topics, services). Resource management is implicit, with no explicit mention of dynamic allocation or load balancing; instead, the architecture assumes **static FE-to-hardware mappings**. Coordination is achieved through **ROS2’s middleware**, which handles inter-entity communication (e.g., sensor data from Physical Twin to Digital Twin for processing). The design diverges from ISO 23247 by **expanding the Physical Twin Entity’s scope** to include general automotive functions, potentially centralizing resource ownership and complicating engineering workflows.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system adheres to the **ISO 23247 Part 2** reference architecture, which defines an **entity-based framework** where the **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)** serves as the digital representation of the **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**. The DTE integrates **functional entities (FEs)** responsible for distinct tasks, such as data transformation into human-interpretable formats, enabling **multi-fidelity modeling** (e.g., geometric, behavioral, or analytical representations of OMEs like equipment, facilities, or processes). While the standard does not prescribe specific model types or data management approaches, it implies **modularity** in model relationships, where FEs may interface with external systems (e.g., **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)**) for data exchange. The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** manages real-time data acquisition from OMEs, ensuring synchronization between physical and digital domains. The architecture supports **interoperable data sources**, though no explicit details on sampling rates, storage mechanisms, or model validation protocols are provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **ISO 23247 Part 2** standard does not explicitly detail **fidelity requirements** (e.g., resolution, granularity) for Digital Twin models or **validation/verification (V&V) methods**. However, it implies **uncertainty quantification** is inherent to the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which must ensure accurate data acquisition from **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** to maintain model validity. The **User Entity** interfaces with the Digital Twin Entity (DTE) for monitoring, suggesting **real-time error detection** via user feedback loops. No specific **quality assurance processes** (e.g., calibration protocols, traceability frameworks) or **error correction mechanisms** (e.g., adaptive model refinement) are documented. The standard’s focus on **entity-based modularity** suggests decentralized fidelity management, but lacks concrete technical specifications for uncertainty sources or V&V methodologies.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
**Data Flows from Physical to Digital:**
  - **Data Types Transmitted:**
    - LiDAR point cloud data (raw and processed).
    - Vehicle state information (e.g., speed, position, orientation).
    - Motor actuation data (e.g., torque, RPM).
    - Steering servo actuation data.
    - Object tracks and clustering results from point cloud processing.
    - Projected driving path (for ACC - Adaptive Cruise Control).
    - Sensor fusion data (e.g., camera input, if applicable).

  - **Communication Protocols:**
    - **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2):** Used for communication between software components within the Digital Twin System (DTS).
    - **Wi-Fi (UDP):** Used for inter-entity communication (e.g., between Physical Twin Entity and Digital Twin Entity).

  - **Transmission Frequencies:**
    - Not explicitly stated in the document, but real-time monitoring implies high-frequency updates (e.g., LiDAR point cloud data may be transmitted at 10-30 Hz, depending on sensor capabilities).

  - **Event Triggers:**
    - Continuous streaming of sensor data (e.g., LiDAR, vehicle state).
    - Triggered updates for specific events (e.g., object detection, path deviation, or user-initiated data collection).

  - **Data Quality Assurance:**
    - **Preprocessing Steps:**
      - **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking:** Raw LiDAR data is processed to cluster points and track objects in the environment.
      - **Data Pre-Processing FE:** Ensures sensor data is cleaned, filtered, and formatted for further processing.
    - **Data Translation:** Serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions (e.g., converting sensor-specific formats to standardized ROS2 messages).

  - **Data Integration Mechanisms:**
    - **Data Collection FE:** Aggregates raw sensor data for storage and reporting.
    - **Reporting FE:** Generates reports from processed data (e.g., vehicle performance, object detection logs).
    - **Presentation FE:** Handles visualization and user interface (UI) data presentation (e.g., 3D visualization of the vehicle and environment).
    - **Data Storage:** Stores processed data for later analysis or retrieval.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
**Control Flows from Digital to Physical:**
  - **Command Types:**
    - **Actuation Commands:** Motor and steering servo control signals (e.g., torque commands, steering angles).
    - **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) Commands:** Speed adjustments, braking, or acceleration commands based on object tracking and path planning.
    - **User Inputs:** Control signals from the user interface (e.g., manual override commands).

  - **Control Message Formats:**
    - Standardized ROS2 message formats for actuation commands (e.g., `std_msgs/Float64` for torque, `geometry_msgs/Twist` for steering).
    - Data Translation component ensures compatibility between message formats across entities.

  - **Validation and Authentication Mechanisms:**
    - Not explicitly described in the document. Assumed to rely on ROS2's built-in message validation (e.g., type checking, range validation for actuation commands).
    - Potential use of digital signatures or access control for critical commands (not mentioned).

  - **Feedback Systems:**
    - **Real-Time Monitoring:** The Digital Twin provides feedback via 3D visualization (e.g., projected driving path, object tracks).
    - **Data Collection FE:** Logs control actions and system responses for analysis.
    - **Reporting FE:** Generates post-mission reports on control performance.

  - **Conflict Resolution:**
    - Not explicitly described. Likely handled by priority-based arbitration (e.g., user override takes precedence over ACC commands).
    - ROS2's multi-master support may allow for distributed control with conflict resolution at the middleware level.

  - **Command Execution Confirmation:**
    - Implicit via sensor feedback loops (e.g., motor actuation commands are confirmed by vehicle state updates from sensors).
    - Potential use of acknowledgment messages in ROS2 (e.g., `ActionServer` feedback for critical commands).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
**Technical Network Infrastructure:**
  - **Communication Protocols:**
    - **ROS2:** Used for intra-entity communication (e.g., between software components within the Digital Twin Entity).
    - **Wi-Fi (UDP):** Used for inter-entity communication (e.g., between Physical Twin Entity and Digital Twin Entity).
    - **Data Translation FE:** Ensures protocol-agnostic data exchange by serializing and converting message formats.

  - **Network Topology and Architecture:**
    - **ROS2 Network:** Centralized or decentralized topology depending on component deployment (e.g., nodes for LiDAR processing, actuation, visualization).
    - **Wi-Fi Network:** Likely a star topology with the Digital Twin Entity as the central hub for sensor data aggregation.
    - **UDP Transport Layer:** Used for low-latency, high-throughput communication between entities.

  - **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements:**
    - **LiDAR Data:** High bandwidth (e.g., 10-100 Mbps for 3D point clouds at 10-30 Hz).
    - **Actuation Commands:** Low latency (<100 ms) to ensure real-time control.
    - **Visualization Updates:** Moderate bandwidth and latency (e.g., 10-30 Hz for 3D rendering).
    - Not explicitly quantified in the document, but real-time constraints imply prioritization of critical data streams.

  - **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms:**
    - **ROS2:** Supports redundant nodes and message retries for fault tolerance.
    - **Wi-Fi:** Potential use of error-correcting protocols or retransmission for UDP packets.
    - **Data Translation FE:** Ensures data integrity through message validation and unit conversion.

  - **Security Protocols:**
    - Not explicitly described. Potential measures:
      - Encryption for Wi-Fi communication (e.g., WPA3 for wireless links).
      - ROS2 security plugins (e.g., authentication for critical nodes).
      - Access control for actuation commands (e.g., role-based permissions).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
**Deployment Infrastructure:**
  - **Hosting Platforms:**
    - **Edge Computing:** Likely deployed on-board the vehicle for real-time processing (e.g., LiDAR clustering, actuation commands).
    - **On-Premise:** Digital Twin Entity may include local servers for data storage and visualization.
    - **Cloud (Not Mentioned):** No explicit mention of cloud hosting, but potential for offloading non-real-time tasks (e.g., analytics, long-term storage).

  - **Computational and Storage Requirements:**
    - **Computational:**
      - High-performance CPU/GPU for point cloud processing (e.g., clustering, tracking).
      - Real-time OS (e.g., ROS2 supports Ubuntu with real-time kernels).
    - **Storage:**
      - Local storage for raw and processed data (e.g., SSDs for fast access).
      - Potential cloud storage for archival or backup (not mentioned).

  - **Scalability Mechanisms:**
    - **Modular Design:** ROS2 nodes can be scaled horizontally (e.g., adding more LiDAR processing nodes for higher resolution).
    - **Dynamic Reconfiguration:** ROS2 supports runtime addition/removal of nodes.
    - **Load Balancing:** Not explicitly described, but potential use of ROS2's multi-master capabilities.

  - **Deployment Models Supported:**
    - **On-Board (Embedded):** For real-time control and sensor processing.
    - **Distributed:** Digital Twin Entity may be split across edge and on-premise systems.
    - **Hybrid (Not Mentioned):** Potential for cloud integration for non-real-time tasks.

  - **Availability and Reliability Assurance:**
    - **Redundancy:** ROS2 supports redundant nodes and failover mechanisms.
    - **Fault Tolerance:** Data Translation FE ensures consistent message formats even if components fail.
    - **Monitoring:** Real-time visualization and reporting provide visibility into system health.
    - **Not Explicitly Quantified:** No SLAs or uptime guarantees mentioned in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin (DT) development process, as outlined in the **ISO 23247 standard (Part 2: Reference Architecture)**, follows an **entity-based approach** where the system is decomposed into four core entities: **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**, **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity**, and **User Entity**. Each entity is further modularized into **Functional Entities (FEs)**, which are responsible for specific tasks such as actuating the OME, transforming data into human-interpretable formats, or interfacing with users. The standard does not prescribe implementation guidelines for FEs, allowing flexibility in methodologies (e.g., using **OPC Unified Architecture** for communication). **Version control** and **evolution management** are not explicitly detailed, but the modular entity structure inherently supports iterative updates and versioning of the DT’s digital representation. **Quality assurance** and **testing** are implied through the structured entity interactions, though no specific validation frameworks (e.g., ISO 9001, TÜV certifications) or testing protocols (e.g., unit/integration testing) are mentioned. The **twinning process** involves mapping physical manufacturing elements (e.g., equipment, processes) to their digital counterparts via the **Digital Twin Entity**, with real-time data synchronization facilitated by the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The **ISO 23247 standard** does not explicitly detail **analytics capabilities** or **decision-making processes** within the Digital Twin (DT) system. However, the **User Entity** provides interfaces for monitoring data and controlling the **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**, implying that insights are generated through **real-time data visualization** and **interactive user dashboards**. The standard does not specify **analytics tools** (e.g., machine learning, predictive maintenance algorithms) or **processing capabilities** (e.g., edge computing, cloud-based analytics). **Decision support** is inferred to rely on **human-in-the-loop validation**, where users interpret data from the **Digital Twin Entity** to make operational decisions. **Confidence measures** for recommendations are not addressed, nor are **automated decision-making workflows** (e.g., rule-based systems, AI-driven suggestions). The lack of explicit details suggests these capabilities are left to implementation-specific solutions.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin (DT) system adheres to the **ISO 23247 standard**, specifically **Part 2: Reference Architecture**, which defines an **entity-based framework** for manufacturing DTs. Key compliance aspects include:
  - **Entity-based Reference Architecture (RA)**: Mandates decomposition into **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**, **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity**, and **User Entity**, each with defined responsibilities.
  - **Interoperability**: While not explicitly stated, the modular design aligns with **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** as a potential communication protocol for data exchange between entities.
  - **Certification Requirements**: The standard does not mandate specific certifications (e.g., ISO 9001, IEC 62443), but compliance with **ISO 23247** ensures adherence to a structured DT development framework.
  - **Compliance Frameworks**: The standard provides a **governance structure** for DT lifecycle management but does not reference external frameworks (e.g., ITIL, COBIT). **Information exchange** (Part 4 of ISO 23247) is implied to support interoperability but lacks technical specifics (e.g., API standards, data formats).
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The Digital Twin system, particularly in the context of **ISO 26262-1:2018 (Road vehicles – Functional safety)** and **ISO 15622:2018 (Intelligent Transport Systems – Adaptive Cruise Control Systems)**, must adhere to **functional safety standards** for safety-critical systems. This includes:
  - **Cybersecurity protocols**: Integration of **authentication systems** and **access control mechanisms** to prevent unauthorized modifications or data breaches, particularly in **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) services** as specified in **3GPP TS 22.185 v15.0.0 (Release 15)**. This standard mandates **secure communication channels** for real-time data exchange between vehicles and infrastructure, ensuring **confidentiality, integrity, and availability** of safety-critical data.
  - **Threat mitigation strategies**: Implementation of **fail-safe mechanisms** and **risk assessment approaches** aligned with **ISO 26262-1**, which defines **Automotive Safety Integrity Levels (ASIL)** for system components. For example, **ASIL D** may apply to critical functions like adaptive cruise control, requiring **redundancy, fault detection, and mitigation** to prevent system failures.
  - **Safety-critical system considerations**: The **model-driven architecture (MDA)** for **interactive digital twin cockpits** (as described in **Dalibor et al., 2020**) emphasizes **real-time synchronization** and **consistency checks** between the physical and digital twin systems. This includes **formal verification methods** to ensure the twin’s behavior aligns with safety requirements, such as **timely response to sensor data anomalies** or **failover protocols** during communication disruptions.
  - **Risk assessment approaches**: The **ISO 26262-1** framework requires **hazard analysis and risk assessment (HARA)** to classify safety goals and allocate **ASIL levels** to system components. For instance, a **Digital Twin for adaptive cruise control** must undergo **safety validation** to ensure its **predictive models** do not introduce unintended risks (e.g., false braking commands).
  - **Authentication systems**: In **V2X services (3GPP TS 22.185)**, **digital certificates** and **secure identity management** are critical for validating entities (e.g., vehicles, roadside units) before data exchange. This prevents **spoofing attacks** or **man-in-the-middle exploits** in cooperative driving scenarios.
  - **Fail-safe mechanisms**: The **Digital Twin must implement **watchdog timers**, **data consistency checks**, and **fallback modes** (e.g., reverting to manual control) if the twin’s predictions deviate beyond predefined thresholds, as per **ISO 15622** requirements for **adaptive cruise control reliability**.
  - **Access control mechanisms**: Role-based access control (RBAC) or **attribute-based access control (ABAC)** may be employed to restrict modifications to the Digital Twin’s **simulation models** or **historical data**, ensuring only authorized personnel (e.g., safety engineers) can update critical parameters.

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

(a) 3D Visualization of the vehicle and its environment (b) Corresponding view from the on-board camera Fig. 3: Visualizations available from the virtual cockpit _C. Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,
